methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Humanoid, a seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Artem Sokolov, has raised $50M to date. The broader Robotics & Manufacturing sector encompasses one public comparable (ticker 277810.KQ) and eight private peers including Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable valuation technique given data constraints. Using total capital raised ($50M) as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.5x multiplier typical of seed-to-next-stage advancement in venture contexts yields $125M. This method directly reflects the company's funding history and market-standard step-up dynamics for the stage.

Peer-median triangulation does not meet evidentiary thresholds. While seven robotics peers exist in available data, same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables each number below three, and similar-valuation-tier matches are insufficient. Applying an all-sector median would introduce material methodological error without robust peer support.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $106M (low), $125M (median), to $144M (high), derived from ±15% variance around the stage-progression point estimate. Key uncertainties include (1) the absence of tightly comparable private transactions at seed stage in this sector, limiting cross-validation, and (2) the single public comparable's relevance to an early-stage private robotics company. This single-method triangulation carries elevated sensitivity to stage-advancement assumptions.
